name: Impersonal "det" (weather expressions)
explanation: In Danish, weather and natural phenomena use "det" as an impersonal subject. This "det" doesn't refer to anything specific — it simply fills the grammatical subject slot. English also does this: "It is raining" = "Det regner".

examples.english: It is very windy in winter.

name: Regular verb conjugation (present tense)
explanation: Most Danish verbs form the present tense by adding -er to the verb stem. Remove the final -e from the infinitive, then add -er. Weather verbs follow this pattern: at regne → regner, at sne → sner, at skinne → skinner.

name: Basic adjective agreement (en/et)
explanation: Danish adjectives change form depending on the noun's grammatical gender. Common nouns (en-words) use the base form; neuter nouns (et-words) usually add -t. After "det er", weather adjectives also take the -t form because "det" is neuter.
